target.name: Artimus Robotics
target.founder: Eric Acome, Shane Mitchell, Nick Kellaris, Christoph Keplinger

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $5M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Artimus Robotics is a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Eric Acome, Shane Mitchell, Nick Kellaris, and Christoph Keplinger. The company has raised $5M+ to date. The valuation universe comprises one public comparable (277810.KQ) and eight private peers in the Robotics & Manufacturing sector, including 1X Technologies, Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, and Bedrock Robotics.

The Stage-Progression method is the only applicable triangulation approach given the data constraints. Using the $5M total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of Seed-to-Series A advancement, this method implies a valuation of $12M. The Peer-Median approach is not applicable; while 11 same-sector comparables exist, the narrow filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier cohorts yielded fewer than three datapoints, insufficient for reliable benchmarking.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $11M to $14M, with a median point of $12M. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. The primary uncertainty is the absence of direct same-stage peer multiples; a broader Series A Seed outcome or a differentiated funding round structure could shift the valuation materially. Additionally, the single applicable method limits triangulation robustness and warrants revisiting this analysis as comparable transaction data in early-stage robotics matures.
